text: The surgical team encounters significant adhesions in the abdominal cavity while dissecting around the left kidney. During the dissection, pus is observed pouring out, and the renal vessels, including what appears to be the renal vein, are identified.
The surgeon dissects the renal vein, clips it with Hem-o-lok clips, and divides it. Subsequently, the renal artery is located, clipped, and divided using the same technique.

The surgeon proceeds with posterior dissection of the kidney, which is adherent to the abdominal wall due to previous surgeries and scarring. During the dissection, a gauze piece is unexpectedly found around the kidney, likely left from an open surgery twenty-two years ago, contributing to recurrent infection and perinephric collection.

The surgeon attempts various energy sources, starting with harmonic, then switching to cautery and hooks due to dense adhesions and charring. The gauze piece around the kidney is divided and removed, followed by careful dissection of remaining gauze attached to the abdominal wall using harmonic.

It's a very rare thing that a patient with gauzy fibroma or forgotten gauze piece will present after twenty two years and it got undetected on multiple ultrasounds as well as CT scan. It was an incidental and surprise finding during the operation that we got.
